{"messages":[{"content":"You are a disciplined wiki maintainer following the Karpathy LLM Wiki pattern.
Output follows Open Knowledge Format (OKF) v0.1 conventions.

## Wiki Schema
# Wiki Schema & Conventions

This wiki follows Open Knowledge Format (OKF) v0.1 conventions.
See https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/knowledge-catalog/blob/main/okf/SPEC.md

## Directory Structure

```
raw/                  Immutable source files (not indexed)
wiki/                 Wiki pages (free-form, no prescribed subdirectory layout)
  index.md            Content catalog (maintained by ingest agent)
  log.md              Chronological record (maintained by ingest agent)
WIKI_SCHEMA.md        This file
.gmd/config.cue       GMD configuration
```

## Naming Conventions

- Use kebab-case for page filenames: `transformer-architecture.md`
- Entity pages: singular noun (`kubernetes.md`, `attention-mechanism.md`)
- Concept pages: descriptive phrase (`scaled-dot-product-attention.md`)
- Comparison pages: `a-vs-b.md` format
- Source pages: `YYYY-MM-DD-short-title.md` format
- Synthesis pages: `YYYY-MM-DD-question-slug.md` format

## Page Format

Every wiki page must:
1. Start with YAML frontmatter (between `---` delimiters)
2. Have a single H1 heading matching the page title
3. Use standard markdown links for cross-references: `[Page Name](/path/to/page.md)`
4. End with a "See Also" section where applicable

## Frontmatter Schema

```yaml
---
type: concept          # REQUIRED by OKF: free-form string
title: Page Title      # OKF recommended: display title
description: Summary   # OKF recommended: auto-generated by summarizer
resource: https://...  # OKF recommended: canonical URI
tags: [tag1, tag2]     # OKF recommended: lowercase, kebab-case
timestamp: 2026-06-13T14:30:00Z  # OKF recommended: ISO 8601, set by tool on write
# GMD extensions:
status: draft          # Free-form: draft, reviewed, needs-update
sources: [page.md]     # Pages this content derives from
---
```

## The Index File (index.md)

The index is the wiki's table of contents. It is maintained by the ingest agent
and should not be manually edited. Format:

```markdown
# Wiki Index

## Entities
* [Entity Name](/wiki/entities/entity-name.md) — One-line description
* ...

## Concepts
* [Concept Name](/wiki/concepts/concept-name.md) — One-line description
* ...

## Comparisons
* [A vs B](/wiki/comparisons/a-vs-b.md) — One-line description
* ...

## Sources
* [Source Title](/wiki/sources/YYYY-MM-DD-title-slug.md) — Summary and date ingested
* ...

## Last Updated
YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM
```

## The Log File (log.md)

The log records every ingest operation chronologically. It is maintained by the
ingest agent and should not be manually edited. Format:

```markdown
# Wiki Log

## [2026-05-31 14:30] ingest | Source Title
- Created: entities/foo.md, concepts/bar.md
- Updated: entities/baz.md
- Flagged contradiction: claim X vs existing page Y

## [2026-05-30 10:15] query-save | "question text"
- Saved: wiki/synthesis/2026-05-30-question-slug.md
```

## Status Lifecycle

```
draft → reviewed → needs-update → reviewed → ...
```

- draft: Initial creation, needs review
- reviewed: Verified for accuracy
- needs-update: New information available, needs revision


## Existing Wiki Pages
---
okf_version: ""
---

# Wiki Index

## Last Updated



## Instructions

Analyze the source content provided by the user. Extract all entities, concepts,
claims, and comparisons. Output a structured JSON response with the following
actions. For each action, specify "create" for new pages, "update" for existing
pages that need modification, or "merge" when content should be added to an
existing page.

For "update" actions, include the new content in "merge_section" (prepend to
section) or "append_content" (append to page).

Every page must:
- Start with YAML frontmatter (type field is REQUIRED)
- Use standard markdown links for cross-references: `[Page Title](/wiki/path/to/page.md)`
- NOT use [[wikilinks]] syntax
- Use kebab-case filenames
- Do NOT include "description" or "timestamp" in frontmatter (these are added automatically)

Choose a concept_kind for each page to guide body structure:
- "entity" — people, orgs, products, technologies
- "concept" — methodologies, architectures, theories
- "comparison" — X vs Y analysis
- "source" — summary of ingested content
- "algorithm" — steps, pseudocode, complexity
- "reference" — definition, glossary
- "tutorial" — prerequisites, steps, examples

For source pages, include a `# Citations` section listing original sources.

Output ONLY valid JSON with this structure:
{
  "source_summary": {
    "title": "Source title",
    "page": "sources/YYYY-MM-DD-title-slug.md",
    "frontmatter": {"type": "source", "tags": [...], "resource": "https://..."}
  },
  "entities": [
    {
      "name": "Entity Name",
      "page": "entities/kebab-case-name.md",
      "action": "create",
      "concept_kind": "entity",
      "content": "# Entity Name\n\n...",
      "frontmatter": {"type": "entity", "tags": [...], "status": "draft"},
      "links_to": ["/wiki/path/to/other.md"],
      "claims": ["specific claim from source"]
    }
  ],
  "concepts": [...],
  "comparisons": [...],
  "contradictions": [
    {
      "claim": "New claim from source",
      "source_page": "sources/...",
      "contradicts_page": "existing-page.md",
      "existing_claim": "What the existing page says",
      "resolution_hint": "How to reconcile"
    }
  ],
  "index_updates": [
    {"page": "entities/foo.md", "summary": "One-line description", "category": "entities"}
  ],
  "log_entry": "## [YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM] ingest | Source Title\n- Created: ...\n- Updated: ...\n- Flagged: ..."
}
","role":"system"},{"content":"# Go Channels

Channels are a fundamental concurrency primitive in Go. They provide a way for goroutines to communicate with each other and synchronize their execution.

## Buffered vs Unbuffered

Unbuffered channels block the sender until a receiver is ready. Buffered channels allow sending up to the buffer capacity before blocking.

## Select Statement

The select statement lets a goroutine wait on multiple channel operations simultaneously. It picks one that is ready randomly.

## Use Cases

Channels are commonly used for:
- Pipeline patterns between goroutines
- Fan-out / fan-in patterns
- Timeout and cancellation signals
- Mutual exclusion via a single goroutine

## Best Practices

Always close channels when done sending. Use range to receive values until the channel is closed. Avoid using channels for pure mutual exclusion — use sync.Mutex instead.","role":"user"}],"model":"Qwen/Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-FP8"}

They abstract away traditional lock-based synchronization, promoting safer and more idiomatic concurrent programming.\n\n## Use Cases\n- Pipeline patterns between goroutines\n- Fan-out / fan-in patterns\n- Timeout and cancellation signals\n- Mutual exclusion (though sync.Mutex is preferred for pure locking)\n\n## See Also\n- [Buffered vs Unbuffered Channels](/wiki/comparisons/buffered-vs-unbuffered-channels.md)\n- [Select Statement](/wiki/concepts/select-statement.md)\n- [sync.Mutex](/wiki/entities/sync-mutex.md)",

      "content": "# Buffered vs Unbuffered Channels\n\nComparison of channel buffering strategies in Go and their synchronization implications.\n\n## Unbuffered Channels\n- **Mechanism**: Blocks the sender until a receiver is ready.\n- **Synchronization**: Enforces strict synchronous communication.\n- **Use Case**: Tight coordination where sending must be acknowledged immediately.\n\n## Buffered Channels\n- **Mechanism**: Allows sending up to the buffer capacity before blocking.\n- **Synchronization**: Provides asynchronous communication up to a defined limit.\n- **Use Case**: Decoupling producer/consumer rates, queuing, and backpressure management.\n\n## Key Differences\n| Aspect | Unbuffered | Buffered |\n|--------|------------|----------|\n| Sender Blocking | Always (until recv) | Only when buffer full |\n| Receiver Blocking | Always (until send) | Only when buffer empty |\n| Coordination Type | Synchronous | Asynchronous (bounded) |\n\n## See Also\n- [Go Channels](/wiki/concepts/go-channels.md)\n- [Select Statement](/wiki/concepts/select-statement.md)",

   ```yaml
   ---
   type: concept
   title: Go Channels
   tags: [golang, concurrency, channels, goroutines]
   status: draft
   ---
   # Go Channels

   Channels are a fundamental concurrency primitive in Go that provide a way for goroutines to communicate with each other and synchronize their execution. They implement a form of Communicating Sequential Processes (CSP).

   ## Overview
   Channels serve as typed conduits through which data is passed between concurrent units of execution (goroutines). They abstract away complex locking mechanisms and promote safe data sharing.

   ## Use Cases
   - Pipeline patterns between goroutines
   - Fan-out / fan-in patterns
   - Timeout and cancellation signals
   - Mutual exclusion (though `sync.Mutex` is preferred for pure cases)

   ## See Also
   - [Buffered vs Unbuffered Channels](/wiki/comparisons/buffered-vs-unbuffered-channels.md)
   - [Select Statement](/wiki/concepts/select-statement.md)
   - [sync.Mutex](/wiki/entities/sync-mutex.md)
   ```

   **3. Comparison: Buffered vs Unbuffered**
   ```yaml
   ---
   type: comparison
   title: Buffered vs Unbuffered Channels
   tags: [golang, concurrency, channels, buffer]
   status: draft
   ---
   # Buffered vs Unbuffered Channels

   Comparison of the two channel types in Go and their blocking behaviors.

   ## Unbuffered Channels
   - **Behavior**: Blocks the sender until a receiver is ready to accept the value.
   - **Synchronization**: Guarantees synchronous communication; sender and receiver are directly coupled.
   - **Use Case**: Strict coordination where sending must be acknowledged immediately.

   ## Buffered Channels
   - **Behavior**: Allows sending up to the specified buffer capacity before blocking.
   - **Synchronization**: Provides asynchronous communication up to the limit. Senders block only when the buffer is full.
   - **Use Case**: Decoupling producer/consumer rates, implementing queues, or managing backpressure.

   ## Key Differences
   | Feature | Unbuffered | Buffered |
   |---------|------------|----------|
   | Blocking Sender | Always (until recv) | Only when buffer full |
   | Blocking Receiver | Always (until send) | Only when buffer empty |
   | Coordination | Synchronous | Asynchronous (bounded) |

   ## See Also
   - [Go Channels](/wiki/concepts/go-channels.md)
   - [Select Statement](/wiki/concepts/select-statement.md)
   ```

   **4. Concept: Select Statement**
   ```yaml
   ---
   type: concept
   title: Select Statement
   tags: [golang, concurrency, channels, control-flow]
   status: draft
   ---
   # Select Statement

   The select statement allows a goroutine to wait on multiple channel operations simultaneously, enabling non-blocking or multi-channel concurrency patterns.

   ## Overview
   A `select` block waits until one of its case statements can proceed. It evaluates all channel operations in a single construct.

   ## Behavior
   - If multiple cases are ready, one is chosen at random.
   - If no cases are ready and a `default` case is present, the `default` executes immediately (non-blocking).
   - If no cases are ready and no `default` exists, the goroutine blocks until one case is ready.

   ## Common Patterns
   - **Multi-channel wait**: Listen on multiple channels for different events.
   - **Timeouts**: Combine with `time.After` to implement deadlines.
   - **Cancellation**: Monitor a `context.Done()` channel alongside work channels.

   ## See Also
   - [Go Channels](/wiki/concepts/go-channels.md)
   - [Buffered vs Unbuffered Channels](/wiki/comparisons/buffered-vs-unbuffered-channels.md)
   ```

{"messages":[{"content":"Generate a single-sentence description (max 150 chars) for this wiki page: # Go Channels

Channels are a fundamental concurrency primitive in Go. They provide a way for goroutines to communicate with each other and synchronize their execution.

## Overview
Channels act as typed conduits that pass data between concurrent execution units (goroutines). They abstract away traditional lock-based synchronization, promoting safer and more idiomatic concurrent programming.

## Use Cases
- Pipeline patterns between goroutines
- Fan-out / fan-in patterns
- Timeout and cancellation signals
- Mutual exclusion (though sync.Mutex is preferred for pure locking)

## See Also
- [Buffered vs Unbuffered Channels](/wiki/comparisons/buffered-vs-unbuffered-channels.md)
- [Select Statement](/wiki/concepts/select-statement.md)
- [sync.Mutex](/wiki/entities/sync-mutex.md)","role":"user"}],"model":"Qwen/Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-FP8"}

request.body:
{"messages":[{"content":"Generate a single-sentence description (max 150 chars) for this wiki page: # Select Statement

The select statement lets a goroutine wait on multiple channel operations simultaneously. It picks one that is ready randomly.

## Behavior
- Evaluates multiple channel send/receive operations concurrently.
- If multiple cases are ready, one is selected at random.
- Blocks if no cases are ready and no `default` case exists.
- Executes immediately if a `default` case is present and no channel operations are ready.

## Common Applications
- Implementing timeouts via `time.After` channels
- Handling cancellation signals alongside work channels
- Multiplexing multiple data streams

## See Also
- [Go Channels](/wiki/concepts/go-channels.md)
- [Buffered vs Unbuffered Channels](/wiki/comparisons/buffered-vs-unbuffered-channels.md)","role":"user"}],"model":"Qwen/Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-FP8"}

request.body:
{"messages":[{"content":"Generate a single-sentence description (max 150 chars) for this wiki page: # Buffered vs Unbuffered Channels

Comparison of channel buffering strategies in Go and their synchronization implications.

## Unbuffered Channels
- **Mechanism**: Blocks the sender until a receiver is ready.
- **Synchronization**: Enforces strict synchronous communication.
- **Use Case**: Tight coordination where sending must be acknowledged immediately.

## Buffered Channels
- **Mechanism**: Allows sending up to the buffer capacity before blocking.
- **Synchronization**: Provides asynchronous communication up to a defined limit.
- **Use Case**: Decoupling producer/consumer rates, queuing, and backpressure management.

## Key Differences
| Aspect | Unbuffered | Buffered |
|--------|------------|----------|
| Sender Blocking | Always (until recv) | Only when buffer full |
| Receiver Blocking | Always (until send) | Only when buffer empty |
| Coordination Type | Synchronous | Asynchronous (bounded) |

## See Also
- [Go Channels](/wiki/concepts/go-channels.md)
- [Select Statement](/wiki/concepts/select-statement.md)","role":"user"}],"model":"Qwen/Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-FP8"}
